    Type to search · ↑↓ to move · Enter to open · Esc to close

    Home › Types › Bombers & experimental giants

    Bombers & experimental giants

    The largest and fastest combat aircraft ever built.

    Aircraft here4
    Longest56.62 m
    Combined built890
    First flights1952–1981
    What it means

    The category, explained

    Strategic bombers push airframe design harder than almost anything else: intercontinental range, heavy loads, and — in some eras — extreme speed. Two of them appear on our records board purely by size.

    The Tu-160 is the longest and heaviest combat aircraft ever constructed. The B-52 has been flying since 1952 and is planned to serve into the 2050s. And the XB-70 Valkyrie, an experimental Mach 3 bomber, remains the fastest very large aircraft ever flown.

    The aircraft

    Every bombers & experimental giant here

    Silhouettes are sized by real wingspan — bigger aircraft draw bigger.

    Boeing B-52
    Tupolev Tu-160
    Rockwell B-1B Lancer
    XB-70

    Boeing B-52 Stratofortress

    Boeing · In service since 1955 — planned to 2050s

    Length48.50 m
    Wingspan56.40 m
    Height12.40 m
    MTOW219,600 kg

    Tupolev Tu-160

    Tupolev · In service — production restarted

    Length54.10 m
    Wingspan (spread)55.70 m
    Wingspan (swept)35.60 m
    Height13.10 m
    Full specification →

    Rockwell B-1B Lancer

    Boeing · Out of production (1988) — in service

    Length44.50 m
    Wingspan41.80 m
    Height10.36 m
    MTOW216,400 kg
    Full specification →

    North American XB-70 Valkyrie

    North American · Preserved — 1 of 2 survives

    Length56.62 m
    Wingspan32.00 m
    Height9.14 m
    MTOW246,365 kg
    Full specification →

    About the data. Every specification is compiled from manufacturer documents and cited per row. Operator fleet counts are approximate snapshots, marked ≈ and dated; individually verified figures carry a ✓. All silhouettes are original works of aircraft.fyi, drawn to true scale. Corrections and enquiries: business@luck.fyi — we fix errors visibly, not silently.
    © 2026 aircraft.fyi
